text:
The diffusion model is a popular model used to uncover underlying psychological processes underlying choice and to make connection with neuroscience using reaction time data. While there are open-source implementations to fit diffusion models, currently these models can only provide point estimates of parameters per subject given the current method typically require large amount of trials per participant and the group level information is ignored. In contrast, hierarchical Bayesian estimation enables the modelling of individual parameter values together with their group-level distribution, and as a by-product provides quantification of parameter uncertainty using posterior distributions.

This work presents a new Python toolbox called HDDM (Hierarchical Drift Diffusion Model) for the rapid and flexible estimation of the drift-diffusion model and for related models of decision-making such as the linear ballistic accumulator. HDDM estimates are based on hierarchical Bayes (HB) models that are given a faster convergence rate of the model estimates as compared to alternative non hierarchical models that need a larger number of trials for each subject. In addition, the estimates that are given by HDDM are based on a Bayesian analysis that is robust to noise. HDDM, as implemented in the toolbox, can be used also to investigate the effect of trial by trial measurement – e.g. fMRI or EEG – on some of the parameters of the drift-diffusion and of related models. The structure of the drift-diffusion model and of the Bayesian analysis for data modeling given by HDDM are explained. An implementation of HDDM is demonstrated using real data for investigation of fast decision-making processes. The reliability of the HDDM implementation is demonstrated through several parameter recovery tests where the HDDM is compared to Maximum Likelihood (ML) and to the χ²-quantile estimation method.

The software and comprehensive documentation are available at: http://ski.clps.brown.edu/hddm_docs/
